items.title: Detections for the Axios supply chain compromise
items.description: A supply chain attack targeting Axios npm package versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4 introduced a malicious transitive dependency (plain-crypto-js@4.2.1) that executed during installation. The attack deploys cross-platform payloads across Linux, Windows, and macOS through a consistent pattern: Node.js spawns OS-native shells to retrieve and execute remote payloads in detached or hidden contexts. Linux vic
items.ai_summary: The Axios supply chain compromise is a technical attack where a malicious transitive dependency is introduced in npm package versions 1.14.1 and 0.30.4, executing during installation and deploying cross-platform payloads. This attack has a real-world impact of allowing threat actors to gain access to systems and execute remote payloads. The most important defender action is to update Axios npm packages to versions outside the affected range.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.001| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Command and Control]:::tactic
    D -->|T1486| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Unmasking The 64-bit Variant of the Infamous Lumma Stealer
items.description: Gen Threat Labs has identified Remus, a new 64-bit infostealer attributed to the Lumma Stealer family, emerging after Lumma's takedown and the doxxing of its alleged core members. First campaigns date back to February 2026, with the malware switching from Steam/Telegram dead drop resolvers to EtherHiding and employing new anti-analysis checks. Remus shares multiple characteristics with Lumma inclu
items.ai_summary: Remus, a 64-bit infostealer, is a new variant of the Lumma Stealer family, employing anti-analysis checks and using EtherHiding for dead drop resolvers. It has been active since February 2026, with campaigns evading detection through virtualization and sandbox evasion. To defend against Remus, prioritize implementing robust anti-evasion measures and monitoring web services for suspicious activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1497| B[Defense Evasion]:::tactic
    B -->|T1102| C[Command and Control]:::tactic
    C -->|T1041| D[Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1082| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Leveling Up with NightSpire Ransomware
items.description: NightSpire ransomware, first discovered in February 2025, presents a categorization challenge regarding whether it operates as Ransomware-as-a-Service (RaaS). Analysis of two incidents from December 2025 and March 2026 reveals significant variations in tactics, techniques, and procedures between attacks. The March 2026 incident involved threat actors installing Chrome Remoting Desktop and AnyDesk
items.ai_summary: NightSpire ransomware poses a significant threat due to its evolving tactics and techniques, including the use of Chrome Remoting Desktop and AnyDesk for remote access. The ransomware's ability to encrypt data for impact, as seen in the T1486 technique, can lead to substantial losses for affected organizations. To defend against NightSpire, organizations should prioritize implementing robust endpoint security measures and monitoring for suspicious remote desktop activity.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1588| B[Obtain Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1486| C[Impact]:::tactic
    C -->|T1219| D[Exploitation for Client Execution]:::tactic
    D -->|T1071| E[Command and Control]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: North Korea's Contagious Interview Campaign Spreads Across 5 Ecosystems, Delivering Staged RAT Payloads
items.description: A North Korean threat operation has published malicious packages across npm, PyPI, Go Modules, crates.io, and Packagist, impersonating legitimate developer tooling. The campaign uses GitHub aliases including golangorg and aokisasakidev to distribute staged malware loaders that contact actor-controlled infrastructure, retrieve payloads from Google Drive, and deliver platform-specific second-stage m
items.ai_summary: The North Korean threat operation compromises software supply chains by publishing malicious packages across multiple ecosystems, delivering staged RAT payloads. This campaign uses social engineering tactics to impersonate legitimate developer tooling, allowing the threat actors to gain remote access to compromised systems. To defend against this threat, developers should verify the authenticity of packages and dependencies before integrating them into their projects.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1071| D[Persistence]:::tactic
    D -->|T1204| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Threat Brief: Widespread Impact of the Axios Supply Chain Attack
items.description: A sophisticated supply chain attack compromised the Axios JavaScript library after threat actors hijacked an npm maintainer account, releasing malicious versions v1.14.1 and v0.30.4. These versions contained a hidden dependency called plain-crypto-js, which deployed a cross-platform remote access Trojan affecting Windows, macOS, and Linux systems. The malware performed reconnaissance, established
items.ai_summary: The Axios supply chain attack involves malicious versions of the JavaScript library, which deploy a cross-platform remote access Trojan, affecting multiple operating systems. This attack performs reconnaissance and establishes remote access, highlighting the need for defenders to monitor third-party libraries and dependencies. Defenders should prioritize verifying the integrity of dependencies and updating to patched versions.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1195| B[Initial Access]:::tactic
    B -->|T1219| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1059.001| D[Persistence]:::tactic

    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: APT28 exploit routers to enable DNS hijacking operations
items.description: Russian cyber actors APT28 have been exploiting routers to overwrite Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP)/Domain Name System (DNS) settings to redirect traffic through attacker-controlled DNS servers. Resulting malicious DNS resolutions enable adversary-in-the-middle (AitM) attacks that harvest passwords, OAuth tokens and other credentials for web and email related services. This puts organi
items.ai_summary: APT28 exploits routers to overwrite DHCP/DNS settings, enabling DNS hijacking operations and adversary-in-the-middle attacks to harvest credentials. This threat has a significant real-world impact, as it allows attackers to intercept sensitive information. To defend against this threat, organizations should prioritize securing their network infrastructure, including routers and DNS servers.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1557| B[Adversary-in-the-Middle]:::tactic
    B -->|T1583.002| C[DNS Server]:::tactic
    C -->|T1584.002| D[DNS Server]:::tactic
    D -->|T1557| E[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Bogus website fakes virus scan, installs Venom Stealer instead
items.description: A fake website impersonating Avast antivirus is tricking users into infecting their computers with Venom Stealer malware. The site runs a fake virus scan, claims to find threats, and prompts users to download a malicious file disguised as a system cleaner. The malware, identified as part of the Venom Stealer family, steals browser credentials, session cookies, cryptocurrency wallets, and other sen
items.ai_summary: The threat is a social engineering attack where a fake website impersonates Avast antivirus, tricking users into downloading Venom Stealer malware. This malware steals sensitive information such as browser credentials and cryptocurrency wallets. The most important defender action is to educate users about the risks of downloading files from untrusted sources and to implement robust antivirus software.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1036| B[Masquerading]:::tactic
    B -->|T1204| C[User Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1555.003| D[Credentials from Web Browsers]:::tactic
    D -->|T1539| E[Steal Web Session Cookie]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: Ringing in Chaos: How TeamPCP Weaponized the Telnyx Python SDK
items.description: TeamPCP uploaded malicious versions of the telnyx Python SDK to PyPI, compromising a package with 750,000 monthly downloads. The attack uses a three-stage architecture: a trojanized package triggers a platform-specific loader, which downloads a second-stage payload hidden in a WAV file using steganography, deploying a credential harvester. The harvester steals various credentials, encrypts them, a
items.ai_summary: TeamPCP compromised the Telnyx Python SDK on PyPI, using a three-stage architecture to deploy a credential harvester. The attack involves a trojanized package, a platform-specific loader, and steganography to hide a second-stage payload. Defenders should update dependencies and monitor for suspicious package downloads.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1608| B[Stage Capabilities]:::tactic
    B -->|T1027| C[Obfuscated Files or Information]:::tactic
    C -->|T1566| D[Initial Access]:::tactic
    D -->|T1059.001| E[Execution]:::tactic
    E -->|T1041| F[Command and Control]:::tactic
    F -->|T1486| G[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: ClickFix Malware Uses macOS Script Editor to Deliver Atomic Stealer
items.description: Jamf Threat Labs discovered a ClickFix-style macOS attack that abuses the applescript:// URL scheme to launch Script Editor and deliver an Atomic Stealer infostealer payload — bypassing Terminal entirely.
items.ai_summary: ClickFix malware abuses applescript:// URL scheme to launch macOS Script Editor, delivering Atomic Stealer infostealer payload without Terminal access. This tactic bypasses traditional security controls, allowing attackers to steal authentication certificates. Defenders should monitor for suspicious applescript:// URLs and block access to Script Editor.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1649| B[Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059.002| C[Execution]:::tactic
    C -->|T1649| D[Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: New Lua-based malware LucidRook observed in targeted attacks against Taiwanese organizations
items.description: Cisco Talos observed a spear-phishing attack delivering LucidRook, a newly identified stager that targeted a Taiwanese NGO in October 2025. The metadata in the email suggests that it was delivered via authorized mail infrastructure, which implies potential misuse of legitimate sending capabilities.
items.ai_summary: LucidRook is a Lua-based malware stager delivered via spear-phishing attacks targeting Taiwanese organizations. The malware leverages phishing (T1566) to gain initial access. Defenders should prioritize monitoring for phishing campaigns and authorized mail infrastructure misuse.

items.title: CVE-2026-5121: A flaw was found in libarchive. On 32-bit systems, an integer overflow vulnerabi...
items.description: A flaw was found in libarchive. On 32-bit systems, an integer overflow vulnerability exists in the zisofs block pointer allocation logic. A remote attacker can exploit this by providing a specially crafted ISO9660 image, which can lead to a heap buffer overflow. This could potentially allow for arbitrary code execution on the affected system.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-5121 is a remote code execution vulnerability in libarchive on 32-bit systems, exploitable via a specially crafted ISO9660 image. An attacker can leverage this flaw to execute arbitrary code on the affected system. Defenders should prioritize patching or mitigating this vulnerability to prevent exploitation.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR
    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|T1203| B[Exploitation for Client Execution]:::tactic
    B -->|T1059| C[Command and Scripting Interpreter]:::tactic
    C -->|T1486| D[Data Encrypted for Impact]:::tactic
    classDef actor fill:#1a0e2e,stroke:#a78bfa,color:#c9d8e8,font-weight:bold
    classDef tactic fill:#0d2038,stroke:#4da6ff,color:#c9d8e8

items.title: CVE-2026-4416: The Performance Library component of Gigabyte Control Center has an Insecure Des...
items.description: The Performance Library component of Gigabyte Control Center has an Insecure Deserialization vulnerability. Authenticated local attackers can send a malicious serialized payload to the EasyTune Engine service, resulting in privilege escalation.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-4416 is an Insecure Deserialization vulnerability in Gigabyte Control Center's Performance Library component. Authenticated local attackers can exploit this vulnerability to achieve privilege escalation. Defenders should update the EasyTune Engine service to prevent exploitation.

items.title: CVE-2026-4415: Gigabyte Control Center developed by GIGABYTE has an Arbitrary File Write vulner...
items.description: Gigabyte Control Center developed by GIGABYTE has an Arbitrary File Write vulnerability. When the pairing feature is enabled, unauthenticated remote attackers can write arbitrary files to any location on the underlying operating system, leading to arbitrary code execution or privilege escalation.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-4415 is an Arbitrary File Write vulnerability in Gigabyte Control Center, allowing unauthenticated remote attackers to write files and potentially execute code or escalate privileges. This vulnerability has a CVSS score of 8.1, indicating a high severity. Defenders should prioritize patching this vulnerability to prevent exploitation.

items.title: CVE-2026-3945: An integer overflow vulnerability in the HTTP chunked transfer encoding parser i...
items.description: An integer overflow vulnerability in the HTTP chunked transfer encoding parser in tinyproxy up to and including version 1.11.3 allows an unauthenticated remote attacker to cause a denial of service (DoS). The issue occurs because chunk size values are parsed using strtol() without properly validating overflow conditions (e.g., errno == ERANGE). A crafted chunk size such as 0x7fffffffffffffff (LONG
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-3945 is an integer overflow vulnerability in tinyproxy's HTTP chunked transfer encoding parser, allowing an unauthenticated remote attacker to cause a denial of service (DoS). This vulnerability can be exploited via T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) and T1498 (Network Denial of Service) tactics. Defenders should prioritize patching tinyproxy to version 1.11.4 or later.

items.title: CVE-2026-2328: An unauthenticated remote attacker can exploit insufficient input validation to ...
items.description: An unauthenticated remote attacker can exploit insufficient input validation to access backend components beyond their intended scope via path traversal, resulting in exposure of sensitive information.
items.ai_summary: CVE-2026-2328 is a remote code execution vulnerability in a public-facing application, allowing an unauthenticated attacker to access backend components and sensitive information. This vulnerability can be exploited via path traversal due to insufficient input validation. Defenders should prioritize patching this vulnerability to prevent exploitation.

items.title: CVE-2026-25704: A Privilege Dropping / Lowering Errors/Time-of-check Time-of-use (TOCTOU) Race C...
items.description:
A Privilege Dropping / Lowering Errors/Time-of-check Time-of-use (TOCTOU) Race Condition vulnerability in  cosmic-greeter can allow an attacker to regain privileges that should have been dropped and abuse them in the racy checking logic.




This issue affects cosmic-greeter before https://github.Com/pop-os/cosmic-greeter/pull/426.

items.title: CVE-2025-15379: A command injection vulnerability exists in MLflow's model serving container ini...
items.description: A command injection vulnerability exists in MLflow's model serving container initialization code, specifically in the `_install_model_dependencies_to_env()` function. When deploying a model with `env_manager=LOCAL`, MLflow reads dependency specifications from the model artifact's `python_env.yaml` file and directly interpolates them into a shell command without sanitization. This allows an attacke

items.title: CVE-2026-5119: A flaw was found in libsoup. When establishing HTTPS tunnels through a configure...
items.description: A flaw was found in libsoup. When establishing HTTPS tunnels through a configured HTTP proxy, sensitive session cookies are transmitted in cleartext within the initial HTTP CONNECT request. A network-positioned attacker or a malicious HTTP proxy can intercept these cookies, leading to potential session hijacking or user impersonation.

items.title: CVE-2026-5107: A vulnerability has been found in FRRouting FRR up to 10.5.1. This affects the f...
items.description: A vulnerability has been found in FRRouting FRR up to 10.5.1. This affects the function process_type2_route of the file bgpd/bgp_evpn.c of the component EVPN Type-2 Route Handler. The manipulation leads to improper access controls. The attack can be initiated remotely. The attack is considered to have high complexity. The exploitability is reported as difficult. The identifier of the patch is 7676

items.title: Number Usage in Passwords: Take Two, (Thu, Apr 9th)
items.description: In a previous diary [1], we looked to see how numbers were used within passwords submitted to honeypots. One of the items of interest was how dates, and more specifically years, were represented within the data and how that changed over time. It is often seen that years and seasons are used in passwords, especially when password change requirements include frequenty password changes. Some examples we might see today:

items.title: New Chaos Variant Targets Misconfigured Cloud Deployments, Adds SOCKS Proxy
items.description: Cybersecurity researchers have flagged a new variant ofmalware called Chaosthat'scapable of hitting misconfigured cloud deployments, marking an expansion of the botnet's targeting infrastructure. "Chaos malware is increasingly targeting misconfigured cloud deployments, expanding beyond its traditional focus on routers and edge devices," Darktrace said in a new report.
items.workflow_graph:
graph LR

items.title: A Little Bit Pivoting: What Web Shells are Attackers Looking for?, (Tue, Apr 7th)
items.description: Webshells remain a popular method for attackers to maintain persistence on a compromised web server. Many "arbitrary file write" and "remote code execution" vulnerabilities are used to drop small files on systems for later execution of additional payloads. The names of these files keep changing and are often chosen to "fit in" with other files. Webshells themselves are also often used by parasitic attacks to compromise a server. Sadly (?), attackers are not always selecting good passwords either. In some cases, webshells come with pre-set backdoor credentials, which may be overlooked by a less sophisticated attacker.
items.workflow_graph:

    A([Threat Actor]):::actor -->|Recon| B[Initial Access]:::tactic

items.title: Russia Hacked Routers to Steal Microsoft Office Tokens
items.description: Hackers linked to Russia's military intelligence units are using known flaws in older Internet routers to mass harvest authentication tokens from Microsoft Office users, security experts warned today. The spying campaign allowed state-backed Russian hackers to quietly siphon authentication tokens from users on more than 18,000 networks without deploying any malicious software or code.

items.title: Germany Doxes “UNKN,” Head of RU Ransomware Gangs REvil, GandCrab
items.description: An elusive hacker who went by the handle "UNKN" and ran the early Russian ransomware groups GandCrab and REvil now has a name and a face. Authorities in Germany say 31-year-old Russian Daniil Maksimovich Shchukin headed both cybercrime gangs and helped carry out at least 130 acts of computer sabotage and extortion against victims across the country between 2019 and 2021.

items.description: A financially motivated data theft and extortion group is attempting to inject itself into the Iran war, unleashing a worm that spreads through poorly secured cloud services and wipes data on infected systems that use Iran's time zone or have Farsi set as the default language.

items.title: Feds Disrupt IoT Botnets Behind Huge DDoS Attacks
items.description: The U.S. Justice Department joined authorities in Canada and Germany in dismantling the online infrastructure behind four highly disruptive botnets that compromised more than three million hacked Internet of Things (IoT) devices, such as routers and web cameras. The feds say the four botnets -- named Aisuru, Kimwolf, JackSkid and Mossad -- are responsible for a series of recent record-smashing distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks capable of knocking nearly any target offline.

items.title: Iran-Backed Hackers Claim Wiper Attack on Medtech Firm Stryker
items.description: A hacktivist group with links to Iran's intelligence agencies is claiming responsibility for a data-wiping attack against Stryker, a global medical technology company based in Michigan. News reports out of Ireland, Stryker's largest hub outside of the United States, said the company sent home more than 5,000 workers there today. Meanwhile, a voicemail message at Stryker's main U.S. headquarters says the company is currently experiencing a building emergency.

items.description: Microsoft Corp. today pushed security updates to fix at least 77 vulnerabilities in its Windows operating systems and other software. There are no pressing "zero-day" flaws this month (compared to February's five zero-day treat), but as usual some patches may deserve more rapid attention from organizations using Windows. Here are a few highlights from this month's Patch Tuesday.

items.title: How AI Assistants are Moving the Security Goalposts
items.description: AI-based assistants or "agents" -- autonomous programs that have access to the user's computer, files, online services and can automate virtually any task -- are growing in popularity with developers and IT workers. But as so many eyebrow-raising headlines over the past few weeks have shown, these powerful and assertive new tools are rapidly shifting the security priorities for organizations, while blurring the lines between data and code, trusted co-worker and insider threat, ninja hacker and novice code jockey.

items.title: Who is the Kimwolf Botmaster “Dort”?
items.description: In early January 2026, KrebsOnSecurity revealed how a security researcher disclosed a vulnerability that was used to assemble Kimwolf, the world's largest and most disruptive botnet. Since then, the person in control of Kimwolf -- who goes by the handle "Dort" -- has coordinated a barrage of distributed denial-of-service (DDoS), doxing and email flooding attacks against the researcher and this author, and more recently caused a SWAT team to be sent to the researcher's home. This post examines what is knowable about Dort based on public information.

items.title: ‘Starkiller’ Phishing Service Proxies Real Login Pages, MFA
items.description: Most phishing websites are little more than static copies of login pages for popular online destinations, and they are often quickly taken down by anti-abuse activists and security firms. But a stealthy new phishing-as-a-service offering lets customers sidestep both of these pitfalls: It uses cleverly disguised links to load the target brand's real website, and then acts as a relay between the target and the legitimate site -- forwarding the victim's username, password and multi-factor authentication (MFA) code to the legitimate site and returning its responses.

items.title: Kimwolf Botnet Swamps Anonymity Network I2P
items.description: For the past week, the massive "Internet of Things" (IoT) botnet known as Kimwolf has been disrupting the The Invisible Internet Project (I2P), a decentralized, encrypted communications network designed to anonymize and secure online communications. I2P users started reporting disruptions in the network around the same time the Kimwolf botmasters began relying on it to evade takedown attempts against the botnet's control servers.
